clean_story:
Hidden messages. Silent assassins.

Feudal espionage tools refers to the devices and methods used by spies, ninja, and samurai in medieval Japan and Europe to gather secrets—often disguised as everyday objects.

A rice bowl’s bottom might hide a coded map, its glaze etched with ink only visible when heated. Shuriken weren’t just weapons; their star shape let them wedge into wood, carrying notes between allies. In Europe, hollowed-out books stored poison or miniature crossbows, while "dead drops"—holes in castle walls—held scrolls wrapped in wax to survive rain.

Ninja used metsubushi, egg-sized clay spheres filled with pepper and iron filings. Thrown, they exploded into blinding smoke, masking escapes. Samurai carried kiseru pipes: their long stems doubled as blowguns for poison darts. Even fans became tools—folded steel ribs could slash throats or signal in Morse-like flicks.

Three related subjects are ninja weaponry evolution, medieval cipher techniques, and how trade routes spread spycraft.
